**Analysis:**  
Alpha-fetoprotein (AFP) is mainly secreted by the fetal liver and passes passively from the bloodstream into the amniotic cavity via diffusion across the chorionic membrane. Because it has very low affinity for plasma proteins and does not bind well to albumin, it can easily diffuse out of the fetal circulation into the amniotic space. In contrast, only small amounts cross into maternal serum due to selective permeability and rapid metabolism; therefore, concentrations remain much lower than those found in amniotic fluid. Thus, among all listed sites—the fetal serum, placenta, amniotic fluid, and maternal serum—amniotic fluid consistently exhibits the highest measured concentration of AFP during pregnancy.

**Answer Choice:** C

Additionally, amniotic fluid lacks systemic dilution factors present in maternal circulation, allowing unfiltered access to fetal-derived proteins.
